The Build vs. Buy Trap

Most product teams approach the build-vs-buy decision with a checklist that almost always tilts toward "buy" — but the checklist is wrong. The real question isn't whether a vendor solution exists; it's whether the capability is core to your competitive moat. If it is, every dollar you spend with a vendor is a dollar funding their roadmap, not yours.

I've seen three patterns in the last decade across roughly forty teams. First, the "buy then regret" cycle — the team buys an off-the-shelf tool because it's faster, then spends two years bolting on customizations that cost more than building from scratch would have. Second, the "build then abandon" failure — the team builds because nothing exists, then pivots and leaves a half-finished system. Third, and rarest, the "build because we must" — the team correctly identifies that the capability is core, builds deliberately, and compounds advantage.

The framework I recommend is simple. Ask three questions. Is the capability a top-three differentiator for our customers? Will we still need to deeply customize this in twelve months? Do we have the engineering capacity to maintain it? If you answer "yes" to all three, build. Otherwise, buy and spend the saved energy on what's actually core.

The trap is that "buy" feels safer in the moment. It's not. Buying non-core is safer; buying core is the most expensive decision a product team can make.
